message.content:
Repo: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-1). Venv: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
/tmp/pass1_reconfirm.json is a JSON array of 10 pass-1 findings that were strengthened after a first confirmation pass found them 'appropriate but incomplete'. Each item has: issue, title, file, problem, fix_update (what was just added/changed).
Constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign, no metered API, engine imports no adapter, lazy optional backends.

You are independent CONFIRMER #1 of 2 for INDEX 8 (0-based) in /tmp/pass1_reconfirm.json.
Read that item (issue, problem, fix_update). Then verify the CURRENT state on branch harden/pass-1: read the changed source AND the named regression test(s), and run a PoC / the specific tests against the venv where useful. Decide: appropriate = the fix soundly addresses the root cause (no hack/over-claim); complete = it fully resolves the finding AND has a regression test that would actually catch a regression (a test that passes equally with/without the fix does NOT count). If the item documents a deliberately-deferred scope (e.g. #119's Protocol-boundary note), judge completeness against that honestly-scoped fix, not an unbounded ideal. Set either false with specifics if not met. Echo the issue number.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the finding at index 8 in the JSON file.

input.command: cat /tmp/pass1_reconfirm.json | python3 -c "import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(json.dumps(d[8], indent=2))"

content.content:
{
  "issue": "121",
  "title": "mypy is pinned to `python_version = \"3.11\"` but CI advertises and matrix-tests up to 3.14, so 3.12\u20133.14-only type errors are never caught",
  "file": "pyproject.toml",
  "sev": "LOW",
  "problem": "`[tool.mypy] python_version = \"3.11\"` forces mypy to type-check the code as if running on Python 3.11 regardless of the runner's interpreter. The core CI lane runs mypy across a 3.11\u20133.14 matrix and the integration lane runs it on 3.12, but because the analysis target is fixed at 3.11, type behavior that differs across versions (e.g. stdlib stubs that gained/changed signatures in 3.12+, version-gated typing constructs) is only ever validated at the 3.11 floor. The project classifies and matrix-t",
  "fix_update": "mypy python_version removed; NEW test test_mypy_is_not_pinned_to_a_single_python_version parses pyproject."
}

content.text: Issue #121. Let me examine the current state on the branch.

content.text: The `python_version` line is removed. Now let me find the regression test.

content.content:
tests/test_packaging.py:38:def test_mypy_is_not_pinned_to_a_single_python_version():
tests/test_packaging.py:39:    # #121: a fixed python_version would hide 3.12-3.14-only type errors that the CI matrix
tests/test_packaging.py:42:    assert "python_version" not in pp.get("tool", {}).get("mypy", {})

1	"""Packaging/config regression guards (pass-1 #120, #121)."""
2	
3	from __future__ import annotations
4	
5	import tomllib
6	from pathlib import Path
7	
8	_PYPROJECT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "pyproject.toml"
9	
10	
11	def _pyproject() -> dict:
12	    return tomllib.loads(_PYPROJECT.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
13	
14	
15	def test_version_is_single_sourced_not_static():
16	    # #120: the version must be declared dynamic (read from __init__.__version__), with no second
17	    # static literal in pyproject that could drift from the module/provenance value.
18	    pp = _pyproject()
19	    assert "version" not in pp["project"], "static version literal reintroduced (drift risk)"
20	    assert "version" in pp["project"].get("dynamic", [])
21	    assert pp.get("tool", {}).get("hatch", {}).get("version", {}).get("path") == (
22	        "src/assay_engine/__init__.py"
23	    )
24	
25	
26	def test_version_matches_installed_distribution():
27	    import importlib.metadata as md
28	
29	    import assay_engine
30	
31	    try:
32	        dist = md.version("assay-engine")
33	    except md.PackageNotFoundError:  # pragma: no cover - only if not installed
34	        return
35	    assert dist == assay_engine.__version__
36	
37	
38	def test_mypy_is_not_pinned_to_a_single_python_version():
39	    # #121: a fixed python_version would hide 3.12-3.14-only type errors that the CI matrix
40	    # is supposed to catch.
41	    pp = _pyproject()
42	    assert "python_version" not in pp.get("tool", {}).get("mypy", {})
43

content.text: Now let me verify the test passes currently, and confirm it would fail if the fix were reverted. Let me also check the CI matrix claim.
Now let me verify the test actually catches a regression by simulating the reverted state, and check the CI matrix claim.

content.text:
CI runs bare `mypy` (no `--python-version` flag), so it inherits the matrix interpreter — exactly what removing the pin enables. The fix is sound and the root cause is addressed.

Let me confirm mypy runs without error under the venv to ensure the fix isn't hiding a now-broken config.

content.content: Success: no issues found in 40 source files

content.text:
Verification complete. mypy runs cleanly under Python 3.14 (the venv interpreter) with no pin — confirming the fix lets mypy infer the target from the running interpreter, validating across the full 3.11–3.14 matrix instead of only the 3.11 floor.

Summary of findings:
- The `python_version = "3.11"` line was removed from `[tool.mypy]` in `/Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/pyproject.toml`, replaced with an explanatory comment (lines 90-94).
- CI invokes bare `mypy` (no `--python-version` flag) across the matrix `["3.11","3.12","3.13","3.14"]` — so removing the pin genuinely causes per-version analysis. Root cause addressed soundly, no hack.
- Regression test `test_mypy_is_not_pinned_to_a_single_python_version` in `/Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/tests/test_packaging.py:38` asserts `python_version` absent. I simulated the reverted state and the assertion fails (test catches the regression). With the fix it passes.
- mypy itself runs successfully under py3.14, confirming the unpinned config is valid.

input.reasoning: Issue #121: mypy was pinned to python_version="3.11", causing all type-checking to target the 3.11 floor even though CI runs a 3.11-3.14 matrix. CURRENT state on harden/pass-1: the python_version line is removed from [tool.mypy] (pyproject.toml lines 90-94, replaced by an explanatory comment referencing #121). This is the correct root-cause fix: mypy now infers the analysis target from the running interpreter. CI confirms it — ci.yml runs bare `mypy` (lines 30, 67) with NO --python-version flag, across matrix python-version ["3.11","3.12","3.13","3.14"], so each lane now type-checks against its own version. The fix is sound, not a hack, no over-claim. COMPLETE: the regression test test_mypy_is_not_pinned_to_a_single_python_version (tests/test_packaging.py:38-42) parses pyproject and asserts "python_version" not in tool.mypy. It passes now; when I simulated the reverted state (reinstating python_version="3.11") the assertion fails, proving the test would actually catch a regression rather than passing equally with/without the fix. I also ran mypy under the venv (Python 3.14): "Success: no issues found in 40 source files", confirming the unpinned config is valid and not masking a broken setup.
input.evidence: pyproject.toml lines 90-94: '[tool.mypy] # No fixed python_version: mypy infers the target from the running interpreter, so the CI matrix (3.11-3.14) type-checks against each version rather than only 3.11 (#121).' — python_version key absent (grep confirms no python_version under [tool.mypy]). tests/test_packaging.py:38-42 regression test: `assert "python_version" not in pp.get("tool", {}).get("mypy", {})`. Test run: 1 passed. Revert simulation: setting pp['tool']['mypy']['python_version']='3.11' makes the same assertion raise AssertionError (test catches regression). .github/workflows/ci.yml:15 matrix python-version ["3.11","3.12","3.13","3.14"]; lines 30 & 67 run bare `mypy` with no version flag. venv mypy run (Python 3.14.5): 'Success: no issues found in 40 source files'.

content.text: Confirmed issue #121 as appropriate and complete.
